title: Ghribia
subtitle: Moroccan almond shortbread that crumbles on the tongue
excerpt: Ghribia are very short, crumbly cookies made from ground almonds, flour, oil and icing sugar. No eggs, no butter: the oil gives them a different, lighter crumble than a butter-based shortbread. They spread and crack attractively as they bake. They are finished with a dusting of icing sugar and keep for two weeks in a tin. The whole batch takes about 45 minutes.
sourceNotes: Ghribia are made throughout Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia, with regional variations in grain and fat. The almond version here is common in Fes and Marrakech. Claudia Roden documents a sesame ghribia in 'A New Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1985). Nargisse Benkabbou includes almond ghribia in 'Casablanca: My Moroccan Food' (2018) as a standard Eid and Ramadan preparation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ghribia keep for up to 2 weeks at room temperature in an airtight tin. Re-dust with icing sugar before serving if needed.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; watch carefully as icing sugar causes rapid browning

glossaryTerms.definition: Shortbread made with vegetable oil rather than butter. Because oil is 100 percent fat with no water, it produces a different crumble from butter-based shortbread: more delicate and lighter in the mouth, but also more fragile to handle before baking. The dough needs to rest for 15 to 20 minutes after mixing to allow the flour to absorb the oil fully; without this rest, the cookies spread unevenly. Oil-based shortbread is common in North African and Middle Eastern baking where ghee or oil are more traditional than butter.

content.text: . The dough will feel softer and less structured than a butter dough; this is correct. Let it rest for 15 minutes before shaping, which allows the flour to absorb the oil and firms it slightly. Handle the shaped cookies gently, as they are fragile before baking.

A teaspoon of orange blossom water adds the fragrance that distinguishes ghribia from a plain almond biscuit. Do not skip it or substitute with vanilla; the flavour is part of the recipe. Find it in Middle Eastern grocery shops or online.

Mix together the 250 g flour, 50 g ground almonds, 100 g icing sugar and 0.5 tsp baking powder. Pour in the 120 ml vegetable oil and 1 tsp orange blossom water and mix with a fork, then with your hands, until a soft dough forms. It should not be sticky but will be quite soft. Cover and rest for 15 to 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough into balls about 2.5 cm across (about 20 g each). Place on the trays spaced 4 cm apart; they will spread slightly. Press a pinch of toasted sesame seeds into the top of each, if using. Flatten very gently with the back of a fork or two fingers.

Bake for 15 to 20 minutes until the cookies are pale gold on top with slightly darker edges, and the surface shows fine cracks. They will still feel soft when they come out; they firm up as they cool. Do not overbake. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes, then carefully transfer to a rack. Dust generously with icing sugar while still warm.

items.problem: Cookies spread into flat, thin discs
items.solution: The dough needed longer to rest, or the oil-to-flour ratio was slightly off. Make sure the dough is rested for the full 15 to 20 minutes, and do not add extra oil. If they spread regardless, refrigerate the shaped balls for 20 minutes before baking.

items.problem: Cookies are very hard and dry after cooling
items.solution: They were overbaked. Ghribia should be removed from the oven when they are still pale and the surface just shows cracks; they look underdone but will set completely as they cool.

items.problem: Dough is too dry and crumbly to roll into balls
items.solution: Add the oil a teaspoon at a time, mixing between additions, until the dough holds together when pressed. Different flour brands absorb oil at different rates.
